vector<string> result;

queue<string> q;

unordered_set<string> visited;

q.push(s);

visited.insert(s);

bool found = false;

while (!q.empty()) {

    int level_size = q.size();

    vector<string> current_level;

    // Collect current level elements
    for (int i=0; i < level_size; ++i) {
        current_level.push_back(q.front());
        q.pop();
    }

    vector<string> valid_in_level;

    // Check each in current level
    for (const string& candidate : current_level) {
        if (is_valid(candidate)) {
            valid_in_level.push_back(candidate);
        }
    }

    if (!valid_in_level.empty()) {
        return valid_in_level;
    }

    // Generate next level
    for (const string& candidate : current_level) {
        for (int i=0; i < candidate.size(); ++i) {
            if (candidate[i] != '(' && candidate[i] != ')') continue;

            string new_candidate = candidate.substr(0, i) + candidate.substr(i+1);

            if (visited.find(new_candidate) == visited.end()) {
                visited.insert(new_candidate);
                q.push(new_candidate);
            }
        }
    }
}

return vector<string>{};

Time complexity: Well, for each level, each possible string is processed. Each string can generate up to O(n) new strings (for each character). But since the maximum length is 25, and the number of parentheses is up to 20, the total possibilities are manageable. However, in worst case, for each level, each string can generate O(n) new strings. But since the BFS stops at the first level with valid strings, the time is acceptable for the constraints.

Time complexity is O(2^n) in worst case, but with n up to 25, but since the problem says maximum 20 parentheses, perhaps O(2^20) is manageable? Well, 2^20 is around a million, but with pruning, perhaps it's acceptable.

But for the problem's constraints, the BFS approach is feasible.

Space complexity is similar, as all generated strings are stored in the queue and visited set.

Now, code in C++:

Implementing the code as discussed.

But in C++, the queue is a std::queue<std::string>, and the visited is a std::unordered_set<std::string> for faster lookups.

So the code:

class Solution {
public:
    vector<string> removeInvalidParentheses(string s) {
        std::queue<string> q;
        std::unordered_set<string> visited;
        q.push(s);
        visited.insert(s);
        vector<string> result;
        
        while (!q.empty()) {
            int levelSize = q.size();
            vector<string> currentLevel;
            for (int i = 0; i < levelSize; ++i) {
                currentLevel.push_back(q.front());
                q.pop();
            }
            
            for (const string& candidate : currentLevel) {
                if (is_valid(candidate)) {
                    result.push_back(candidate);
                }
            }
            if (!result.empty()) {
                return result;
            }
            
            for (const string& candidate : currentLevel) {
                for (int i = 0; i < candidate.size(); ++i) {
                    if (candidate[i] != '(' && candidate[i] != ')') continue;
                    string newCandidate = candidate.substr(0, i) + candidate.substr(i + 1);
                    if (visited.find(newCandidate) == visited.end()) {
                        visited.insert(newCandidate);
                        q.push(newCandidate);
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        
        return vector<string>{};
    }
    
private:
    static bool is_valid(const string& s) {
        int balance = 0;
        for (char c : s) {
            if (c == '(') {
                ++balance;
            } else if (c == ')') {
                --balance;
            }
            if (balance < 0) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return balance == 0;
    }
};

Wait, but in the code above, after processing each level, we check if any valid strings are found. If yes, return. Otherwise, proceed. This ensures that the first occurrence (minimum removals) is returned.

But what about duplicates in the result? For example, same string added multiple times. But the visited set ensures that each string is processed once. So the result vector may have duplicates? No, because in the currentLevel, each candidate is unique. Because when adding to the queue, we check visited. So currentLevel contains unique strings. Then, when checking for validity, if multiple same candidates are present (unlikely due to visited set), they are processed once. So the result will not have duplicates.

Testing example 1: the code returns ["(())()", "()()()"], which matches the sample.

Another example: input "(a)())()". The valid outputs are ["(a())()", "(a)()()"].

So the code should generate these.

Now, what about the case where the same string is generated via different paths but same string? For example, removing different parentheses but same result. The visited set ensures that it's processed once.

Now, the code seems correct.

Now, time and space complexity.

Time complexity: O(N * 2^N) in worst case. But for N up to 25, but with pruning, perhaps it's manageable. However, for each level, each string is processed once. Each string can generate O(N) new strings. But since the maximum depth is O(N), the time complexity is O(N * 2^N). But for N up to 25, this is not feasible. However, given the constraints that there are at most 20 parentheses, perhaps the actual number is manageable. For example, each step removes one parenthesis, and the maximum number of steps is 20. So for each level, the number of possibilities is O( (20 choose k) ), where k is the number of removals. But since BFS stops at the first level with valid strings, perhaps it's efficient enough.

But for the problem's constraints, this approach is acceptable.

Time complexity: O(N * 2^N) in worst case. But since N is up to 25, but with the problem constraints (max 20 parentheses), perhaps it's acceptable.

Space complexity: O(2^N) in worst case, as the visited set can hold all possible generated strings.

But for the problem's constraints, it's manageable.

Efficiency: MEDIUM? Or HIGH? Well, BFS ensures that we find the minimal removals quickly. However, in worst case, it's exponential. But given the problem constraints, it's acceptable. So efficiency is MEDIUM.

Energy implications: LOW, since for the given constraints, the code runs efficiently.

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    vector<string> removeInvalidParentheses(string s) {
        std::queue<string> q;
        std::unordered_set<string> visited;
        q.push(s);
        visited.insert(s);
        vector<string> result;
        
        while (!q.empty()) {
            int levelSize = q.size();
            vector<string> currentLevel;
            for (int i = 0; i < levelSize; ++i) {
                currentLevel.push_back(q.front());
                q.pop();
            }
            
            for (const string& candidate : currentLevel) {
                if (is_valid(candidate)) {
                    result.push_back(candidate);
                }
            }
            if (!result.empty()) {
                return result;
            }
            
            for (const string& candidate : currentLevel) {
                for (int i = 0; i < candidate.size(); ++i) {
                    if (candidate[i] != '(' && candidate[i] != ')') continue;
                    string newCandidate = candidate.substr(0, i) + candidate.substr(i + 1);
                    if (visited.find(newCandidate) == visited.end()) {
                        visited.insert(newCandidate);
                        q.push(newCandidate);
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        
        return vector<string>{};
    }
    
private:
    static bool is_valid(const string& s) {
        int balance = 0;
        for (char c : s) {
            if (c == '(') {
                ++balance;
            } else if (c == ')') {
                --balance;
            }
            if (balance < 0) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return balance == 0;
    }
};
```
efficiency: MEDIUM
time complexity: O(N * 2^N)
space complexity: O(2^N)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses BFS to explore all possible strings by removing one parenthesis at each level. It ensures the minimum removals by returning the first valid level encountered. The visited set prevents redundant checks. Strengths include correctness and minimal removals guarantee. Weaknesses are high time/space complexity for worst-case scenarios.
